metrics.narrative: The buyer's pain is quantified in hard operational terms: procurement handles 87 to 100 contracts a year and roughly 30 to 50 RFQs a week, entirely by email, PDF and Excel tracker (call, 16 Jan 2026, Kerolos Abdelmalk). Kerolos stated he intends to build his own internal business case by pricing the risk in each broken process against Zycus's cost ('in our procurement process, we have risk in one, two, 3, 4, and we can cover this risk through this system, and the cost of this system is x', call, 30 Jan 2026). Zycus supplied its own generic benchmark stats (roughly 20% faster sourcing, 30% faster contract review, 60% less manual AP work) to help that case, but no buyer-confirmed ROI number or approved business case exists yet.

champion.narrative: Kerolos Abdelmalk self-initiated the engagement after meeting a Zycus representative at an industry event, drove both discovery calls, and is personally building the internal business case (calls, 16 and 30 Jan 2026; MEDDPICC_2_0__c). Aygul Gurbanova, Senior Procurement Officer, is a secondary, engaged participant on both calls. However there is no evidence Kerolos has secured an internal sponsor above himself, and the deal owner's own Next Step rates confidence at just 10% as of 2 Jul 2026, with feedback overdue against the buyer's own end-June target — a champion who is willing but not yet demonstrated to be moving the deal internally.

competition.narrative: Salesforce's Competitors field is blank and CRM MEDDPICC 2.0 states 'not clear at this stage.' Kerolos confirmed on the 16 Jan 2026 call that he is running a multi-vendor process ('I met with a lot of providers who can automate this') and a light RFP was submitted into that field on 22 May 2026 (Next_Step__c), but no competitor has ever been named in either call transcript, Next Step, Next Step History, or the CRM. This is a genuine, unresolved knowledge gap, not a hygiene issue.

identify_pain.narrative: Kerolos described the pain comprehensively and personally on both calls: contracts are handled 'a to z manually' through email, PDF and shared Excel trackers with no change-tracking or audit trail and no renewal/milestone alerts (creating late-payment risk); 30 to 40 (up to ~50) RFQs open weekly with no system to track outcomes or supplier performance; and Oracle Fusion, purchased at the Australia-parent level, was never scoped with a contract module for the Dubai campus.

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics have started. Zycus shared a Statement of Work for CLM and Sourcing on 1 May 2026 and asked the buyer to review and confirm or decline participation; there is no evidence of legal review, redlining, or signature discussion anywhere in the record. The SOW's coverage narrowing to 'CLM & Sourcing' (versus the three modules discussed on 30 Jan 2026 — CLM, sourcing, and supplier risk) suggests a possible scope reduction rather than progress toward paper.

economic_buyer.narrative: Salesforce's MEDDPICC 2.0 (last updated 2026-05-15) names 'Kerelos HOP' as the economic buyer, but Kerolos directly and repeatedly contradicts this on both discovery calls: he has no budget authority of his own and must win Dubai management approval before escalating to the University of Wollongong Australia parent (and ultimately the global CFO) for funding ('It would be approved based on the business case'; 'they will give me budget', call, 30 Jan 2026). A global CFO visit in January 2026 encouraged automation generally, but that person has never engaged with Zycus directly. Per this engine's rule that fresher, contradicting call evidence overrides a stale CRM checkbox, the true economic buyer is treated as unidentified and unengaged.

decision_process.narrative: The process is explicit: Kerolos collects proposals from all vendors, builds a business case comparing technical fit, submits it to Dubai management, and Dubai then escalates to the Australia parent for budget release (calls, 16 and 30 Jan 2026; MEDDPICC_2_0__c). That plan has slipped: a business case originally targeted for end-February 2026 was delayed by a regional conflict-related disruption (Next_Step__c, 1 May 2026: 'Project delayed by the war'); a light RFP was only submitted 22 May 2026; and the buyer's own promised feedback-by-end-June (Next_Step__c, 4 Jun 2026) has now passed with no confirmation as of the owner's follow-up on 2 Jul 2026.

decision_criteria.narrative: Five criteria are well documented and corroborated across both calls and the CRM: mandatory Oracle Fusion integration so AP can match invoices/payments against contract milestones; a genuine end-to-end sourcing-to-contract flow (RFQ, scoring, contract, milestone, payment gating) rather than a bare CLM repository; AI-assisted clause detection and red-flagging; itemised per-module pricing to support a phased internal business case; and fit within a small deployment (roughly 7 power users plus about 17 approval managers, Dubai campus only).

Hakim has picked up the account history and is now asking Kerolos to share availability for a re-engagement meeting - so the ball is in UOWD's court to schedule, and the deal is essentially restarting relationship-building after the account handoff.

The last real two-way conversation with the buyer was the discovery call on 30 Jan 2026 (roughly five and a half months ago); since then the relationship has run entirely through email, most recently an incoming reply from the buyer on 2 Jun 2026 confirming they were still evaluating.

The buyer explicitly says they have budget available (22 May 2026), even though the university's own two-tier funding approval, through Dubai management and then the Australia parent, has never been confirmed as cleared.

On 2 Jul 2026 (about a week ago) the account owner sent a follow-up to the champion plus a separate introductory email referencing a contact noted only as 'Hakim' in the deal log; who this is and why they were introduced could not be verified from any source read.

The buyer's own 4-step process (Kerolos collects proposals -> business case -> Dubai management approval -> Australia HQ budget release) targeted business case submission by end-Feb 2026 and Australia sign-off by Mar 2026; the 4 Jun and 2 Jul Next Step updates show neither is confirmed, and the end-June provider-feedback deadline passed silently. flags: Surface the competitive shortlist and build the wedge now. At Formal Evaluation in a multi-vendor RFP (confirmed 22 May 'light RFP submitted') with unknown competition, Zycus is flying blind. Not knowing who we are up against or how we compare on fit/cost is a critical gap 35 days from close. Next move: on the re-engagement call, ask Kerolos candidly 'who else are you evaluating, and how do we stack up on Oracle integration and contract-risk fit versus them?' Use the answer to sharpen the wedge: emphasize Zycus's Oracle Fusion customer base ('dozens of multibillion-dollar customers' per Dan 30 Jan), the contract lifecycle + AI-assisted redlining fit against his stated pain (manual contracts = high risk of late payment and missed renewals), and the multi-campus expansion potential (Dubai success → Malaysia/Hong Kong/Australia/India rollout, which justifies the investment and aligns with the global CFO's automation mandate). If Kerolos names a lower-cost competitor, the countermove is ROI and risk mitigation (lower upfront cost but higher operational risk and manual toil) plus the Australia parent precedent (Oracle ERP was a cross-campus enterprise buy, CLM/sourcing should be too).

No champion: Kerolos is friendly but not advocating internally; no evidence of EB introduction, competitive intelligence sharing, or proactive deal-driving. 10% confidence rating and 27-day silence suggest deal is passive or deprioritized.

Buyer silent 27 days past expected feedback deadline: Next Step 4 Jun expected end-June feedback; today is 29 Jun with no buyer contact since 2 Jun. Rep outreach 4 Jun awaiting reply. Deal may be stalled or deprioritized.

Close date 31 Jul (32 days) not credible; stage is Formal Evaluation, no business-case approval, no EB engagement, no contract/legal/InfoSec process started; realistic timeline from approval to signature is 60-90 days, suggesting Q3 or Q4 2026 close more likely

Extract competitive intelligence and position the wedge before the RFP closes. Zero competitors named 24 days from close despite a confirmed multi-vendor RFP — we are flying blind. Kerolos's five decision criteria (Oracle integration, S2C workflow, AI clause detection, per-module pricing, user-count fit) suggest he is comparing integrated S2C platforms, likely Ivalua, Coupa CLM, SAP Ariba, GEP, or regional vendors who can undercut Zycus on price. Dan flagged UOWD is 'towards the smaller end' and may find Zycus expensive; Kerolos stated 'if budget is constrained my priority is contracts' — if a rival offers standalone CLM at $80k–100k vs. Zycus $150k–200k bundled, we lose. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos and ask directly: who else is in the RFP, what are they proposing, how does Zycus compare? Then position our wedge (Merlin AI, best-in-class CLM, Oracle customer precedent with dozens of blue-chip universities) against the specific shortlist. If Kerolos won't share, that signals weak championship and we should prepare for loss or no-decision.

Multi-thread past Kerolos into the actual budget layer (Dubai management, Global CFO, Australia parent) before the 31 Jul close date, since Kerolos has repeatedly said he holds no budget authority himself.
Close the still-open SOW confirm/decline loop from 1 May 2026 explicitly -- it is the qualification gate that precedes any real RFP evaluation outcome.
Re-engage Kerolos now that the end-June feedback deadline has passed silently; naming the missed date directly will surface whether the business case died internally or is simply delayed.

Get a named contact above Kerolos (Dubai management or the Australia HQ budget owner) onto a call; at $135k this is a realistic ask, and until it happens Zycus has zero direct signal on approval odds.

Deal-size mismatch risk: Zycus's own regional VP flagged (30 Jan 2026) that this deal sits toward Zycus's minimum threshold and that Zycus does not sell single modules — keep per-module pricing sharp and economies-of-scale framing front and center against smaller modular rivals.

value.flag: Surface the competitive shortlist and build the wedge now. At Formal Evaluation in a multi-vendor RFP (confirmed 22 May 'light RFP submitted') with unknown competition, Zycus is flying blind. Not knowing who we are up against or how we compare on fit/cost is a critical gap 35 days from close. Next move: on the re-engagement call, ask Kerolos candidly 'who else are you evaluating, and how do we stack up on Oracle integration and contract-risk fit versus them?' Use the answer to sharpen the wedge: emphasize Zycus's Oracle Fusion customer base ('dozens of multibillion-dollar customers' per Dan 30 Jan), the contract lifecycle + AI-assisted redlining fit against his stated pain (manual contracts = high risk of late payment and missed renewals), and the multi-campus expansion potential (Dubai success → Malaysia/Hong Kong/Australia/India rollout, which justifies the investment and aligns with the global CFO's automation mandate). If Kerolos names a lower-cost competitor, the countermove is ROI and risk mitigation (lower upfront cost but higher operational risk and manual toil) plus the Australia parent precedent (Oracle ERP was a cross-campus enterprise buy, CLM/sourcing should be too).

value.flag: Extract competitive intelligence and position the wedge before the RFP closes. Zero competitors named 24 days from close despite a confirmed multi-vendor RFP — we are flying blind. Kerolos's five decision criteria (Oracle integration, S2C workflow, AI clause detection, per-module pricing, user-count fit) suggest he is comparing integrated S2C platforms, likely Ivalua, Coupa CLM, SAP Ariba, GEP, or regional vendors who can undercut Zycus on price. Dan flagged UOWD is 'towards the smaller end' and may find Zycus expensive; Kerolos stated 'if budget is constrained my priority is contracts' — if a rival offers standalone CLM at $80k–100k vs. Zycus $150k–200k bundled, we lose. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos and ask directly: who else is in the RFP, what are they proposing, how does Zycus compare? Then position our wedge (Merlin AI, best-in-class CLM, Oracle customer precedent with dozens of blue-chip universities) against the specific shortlist. If Kerolos won't share, that signals weak championship and we should prepare for loss or no-decision.

value.flag: Co-author the quantified business case and convert Kerolos from passive contact to active champion. Kerolos articulated qualitative pain comprehensively but has not quantified the ROI — no dollar savings, FTE reduction, or cycle-time improvement to justify $150k–200k to the Dubai CFO and Australia parent. Dan offered typical savings percentages (20% sourcing cycle reduction, 30% faster contract review, 15% supplier risk reduction) but Kerolos has not circled back, and the 10% confidence rating plus 35-day buyer silence suggest the business case is stalled or deprioritized. A passive contact becomes a champion when they co-own the business case and sell it internally. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos, map his 87–100 annual contracts and 30–40 weekly RFQs to Zycus benchmarks, translate qualitative pain into defendable ROI numbers, and co-author the business-case narrative he presents to Dubai + Australia. That co-authorship builds championship and keeps Zycus competitive in the RFP.
